1: Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman (kol-chakham) in whom YHWH has put skill and intelligence (chakhmah u'tevunah): The combination chakhmah (wisdom/skill) + tevunah (understanding/discernment, from bin = to discern) is the Solomonic wisdom-pair applied to artisanship. All skilled work has the same divine source as prophetic speech; the Spirit (ruach) who fills Bezalel for craftsmanship (31:3) is the same Spirit that equips prophets. The biblical tradition makes no hierarchy between artistic and prophetic gifts.
2: Moses called (vayyiqra) Bezalel and Oholiab and every craftsman in whose heart (libo) YHWH had put skill: The heart (lev) is the seat of both wisdom and volition in Hebrew anthropology; chakhmah placed in the lev = integrated knowledge that operates through the whole person, not merely intellectual competence. Everyone whose heart (kol asher nasa'o libo) stirred him up to come to do the work: Nasa' lev = the heart being lifted/stirred — voluntary motivation from within, not conscription.
3: They received from Moses all the contribution (kol-hat'rumah) that the people of Israel had brought: T'rumah = a contribution lifted up/offered (from rum, to raise); the term for the freewill offering to YHWH. And they still kept bringing him freewill offerings (n'davah) every morning (baboqer baboqer): N'davah = freewill offering (from nadav, to impel freely; the same root as Nadab); the double baboqer (morning morning) emphasizes daily consistency. The people give without being asked, every single morning.
4: So all the craftsmen who were doing every sort of task on the sanctuary came, each from the task he was doing: The skilled workers interrupt their own construction work to deliver a message to Moses — the supply has exceeded the demand. The redundancy of having workers stop work to report excess work is itself a picture of extravagant generosity.
5: The people bring much more than enough (yeter) for doing the work that YHWH has commanded us to do: Yeter = surplus/excess/remainder (from yatar, to remain after completion); the unusual situation of a building campaign where the contributions must be stopped rather than solicited. This is the only place in the OT where Moses must restrain giving rather than urge it. The yeter is what Paul is invoking in 2 Cor 9:7 and 8 — the overflow from cheerful giving.
6: So Moses commanded, and word was proclaimed throughout the camp, 'Let no man or woman do anything more for the contribution for the sanctuary': The proclamation to stop giving (lo ya'asu od m'lacha l't'rumat haqqodesh) is the most remarkable fundraising announcement in history. The people are told to put their money away. So the people were restrained from bringing: Vayyikale ha'am mehavi = the people were held back from bringing; kalah = to hold back/prevent/contain.
7: For the material they had was sufficient to do all the work, and more (vayyoter): Vayyoter = and it remained over/was surplus; the same yater root. The tabernacle's construction fund had a surplus, not a deficit. The theological contrast with every subsequent Temple project (both require hard fundraising) marks the Sinai generation as uniquely generous — or uniquely obedient to the Spirit's prompting.
8: All the craftsmen among the workmen made the tabernacle (hamishkan) with ten curtains of fine twined linen (shesh moshzar): Mishkan = dwelling/tabernacle (from shakan, to dwell — the root of Shekinah); the first layer of the structure. Shesh moshzar = fine twisted/spun linen (moshzar from shazar, to twist thread); blue (t'khelet), purple (argaman), and scarlet (tola'at shani) — the three colors of the high-priestly garments, the Temple veil, and ultimately the symbolism of royalty, priesthood, and sacrifice. Cherubim skillfully worked (ma'aseh choshev): Choshev = a designer/thinker (from chashav, to think/devise); the highest weaving designation in the tabernacle — designs woven in by a thinking craftsman, not merely embroidered on top.
9: The length of each curtain was twenty-eight cubits: The precise dimensions (28 cubits long × 4 cubits wide) reflect the 'pattern of the mountain' (25:9) — not rounded estimates but exact replication of what Moses saw. The architectural exactness of the construction narrative (chapters 36-40) is the formal proof that Israel built what YHWH commanded; the repetition of the commands in chapters 25-31 followed by the execution in chapters 36-40 is a deliberate structural device showing obedient correspondence.
10: He coupled five curtains to one another, and the other five curtains he coupled to one another: The ten curtains are divided into two sets of five, joined by loops and clasps to form one structure — unity achieved through precise joining, not monolithic construction. The two halves (five + five) become one over the meeting point of the ark.
11: He made loops of blue (lulaaot t'khelet) on the edge of the outermost curtain of the first set: Lulaaot = loops (from lul = a spiral/loop); the t'khelet blue loops on the edges of each set align the two halves precisely. The loop-and-clasp system (50 loops × 50 clasps) is the technical connection mechanism of the tabernacle's first layer.
12: Fifty loops were on the one curtain, and fifty loops were on the edge of the curtain that was in the second set; the loops were opposite one another: The precise alignment of 50 loops to 50 loops — each loop on the first panel directly faces a corresponding loop on the second panel. The architectural care for alignment reflects the larger principle: the tabernacle is a unified structure, not a collection of independent parts.
13: And he made fifty clasps of gold (qarsei zahav) and coupled the curtains one to the other with clasps: Qeres = clasp/hook (a rare term; also used in 26:6); the gold clasps join the two sets of ten curtains into one mishkan. Gold clasps at the center of the structure — the joining point directly over the ark — are the most precious connectors. So the tabernacle became one whole (vayehi hamishkan echad): the unity formula; one mishkan from the joining of two sets.
14: He also made curtains of goats' hair (iz'zim) for a tent (ohel) over the tabernacle: The ohel (tent) is the second layer over the mishkan; iz'zim = goats (plural of ez = goat); the coarser goat-hair fabric forms the working tent that protects the fine linen below. The layered construction (mishkan + ohel + ram skin covering + dugong/seal skin covering) creates concentric protective zones around the divine dwelling — beauty hidden under increasingly utilitarian exteriors.
15: The length of each curtain was thirty cubits and the breadth four cubits: The goat-hair curtains are slightly longer than the linen curtains (30 vs. 28 cubits) so they overhang the sides and back of the structure. The careful calculation of overhang (36:13-14 in the original Sinai instructions) is now executed precisely.
16: He coupled five curtains by themselves and six curtains by themselves: The goat-hair layer has eleven curtains (vs. ten for the linen), divided into groups of five and six. The extra curtain (the sixth in the group) provides the additional length needed for the front overhang that 'folds back' (v.29) over the entrance.
17: He made fifty loops on the edge of the outermost curtain of the one set and fifty loops on the edge of the curtain of the other set: The same loop-and-clasp system as the linen layer, now with bronze clasps instead of gold — the outer layer uses bronze (lesser value), the inner layer gold. The material gradient signals gradations of holiness from periphery to center.
18: And he made fifty clasps of bronze (qarsei n'choshet) to couple the tent together that it might be one whole: N'choshet = bronze/copper — the metal of judgment and durability, used for the outer covering and the court. The tent (ohel) is joined with bronze clasps as the mishkan was joined with gold clasps; same structure, different material level.
19: He made for the tent a covering of tanned rams' skins (oroth eilim m'adammim) and a covering of goatskins (oroth t'chashim) on top: The two outer coverings — tanned red rams' skins and t'chash skins (the exact animal is debated: dugong? badger? dolphin? the t'chash is otherwise unknown; some suggest a type of fine leather) — are purely protective, not decorative. The beauty of the inner linen curtains with their cherubim is invisible from outside; the tabernacle appears from the outside as a tent of rough hides.
20: Then he made the upright frames (q'rashim) for the tabernacle of acacia wood (atzei shittim): Q'rashin (also transliterated q'rashim) = planks or frames — the structural skeleton of the tabernacle; shittim = acacia (the hard, durable desert wood). The q'rashim are overlaid with gold: incorruptible hard wood covered in pure gold — a material description that early Christian interpreters read as the two natures of Christ (the human/wood nature covered by/united with the divine/gold nature), though the primary function is architectural durability and portability.
21: Ten cubits was the length of a frame, and a cubit and a half the breadth of each frame: The precise dimensions of each q'reish; the 10-cubit height determines the wall height of the tabernacle. The measurement system (cubit = roughly 18 inches) gives the tabernacle dimensions: approximately 15 feet high, 45 feet long, 15 feet wide — a modest structure by temple standards, deliberately mobile.
22: Each frame had two tenons (yadot sh'tayim), for fitting together: Yadot = hands/tenons (from yad = hand; the protrusion at the base that fits into the silver socket like a hand fitting into a sleeve); sh'tayim = two. Each frame fits into two silver bases, giving it a double-footed stability. The yad-vocabulary for structural joints continues the body-metaphor throughout Exodus's architectural vocabulary.
23: He made twenty frames (q'rashim) for the south side of the tabernacle: Twenty frames × 1.5 cubits each = 30 cubits of wall length on the south side — the total southern wall. The systematic enumeration of every frame and socket through vv.23-30 reflects the legal precision of the construction account: what was commanded is now documented as executed.
24: And forty bases of silver (adnei-kesef) he made under the twenty frames, two bases under one frame for its two tenons: Adon = base/socket/pedestal (from adon = lord? or from the root for foundation?); adnei-kesef = silver bases. Each frame sits in two silver sockets (one per tenon); 20 frames × 2 sockets = 40 silver bases on the south side. The silver bases are made from the census silver (38:27) — the poll-tax half-shekel from every Israelite man. Every Israelite's ransom payment becomes a part of the foundation on which the dwelling of YHWH rests.
25: For the second side of the tabernacle, on the north side, he made twenty frames: The north wall mirrors the south wall — same number of frames, same silver base count. The architectural symmetry of the tabernacle reflects the divine character: ordered, consistent, precise. The repetition of dimensions is not literary padding but the language of covenant faithfulness — what was promised is what was built.
26: And their forty bases of silver: two bases under each frame: The north wall's 40 silver bases = the south wall's 40 bases; total for both long walls = 80 silver bases. The structural foundation of the tabernacle rests on 100 silver talent-worth of bases (38:27): 96 for the walls + 4 for the veil pillars. Each talent of silver = the ransom price for 3,000 men (at a half-shekel each) = the entire 603,550-man census covered.
27: For the rear of the tabernacle westward he made six frames: The western (rear) wall uses six regular frames plus two corner frames (vv.27-28); the rear wall is 9 cubits wide (6 × 1.5), plus the doubled corner posts that close the structure. The tabernacle faces east (the entrance faces east, toward the rising sun and the camp), and its rear faces west — the Most Holy Place is in the western end.
28: He made two frames for corners of the tabernacle in the rear: The corner frames (mikzoa = corners, from qatza = to cut off/corner) are the structural joints that close the rectangular structure. The doubled construction ('coupled beneath and together at the top to the first ring') creates a boxed corner — the corner posts are paired to ensure the frame holds its shape under the weight of the coverings.
29: And they were separate beneath but joined at the top, at the first ring: The corner frames are distinct at the base but converge at the top ring — the unity at the top despite structural separation at the base. The ring (ta'ba'at) at the top is the structural linchpin that ties the wall panels into a continuous perimeter.
30: Thus there were eight frames with their bases of silver: sixteen bases, under every frame two bases: The complete western wall: 8 frames (6 regular + 2 corner) × 2 silver bases = 16 silver bases. Total silver bases: 40 (south) + 40 (north) + 16 (west) = 96, plus 4 for the inner veil pillars = 100 silver bases = 100 silver talents (one talent per base, 38:27).
31: He made bars (b'richim) of acacia wood, five for the frames of the one side of the tabernacle: B'richim = crossbars/bars (from barach = to flee/pass through; the bars pass through rings on the frames, running horizontally through the walls). Five bars per side × 3 sides = 15 bars total. The bars tie the frames together horizontally, preventing lateral movement; the vertical frames + horizontal bars create a rigid grid.
32: And five bars for the frames of the other side of the tabernacle, and five bars for the frames of the tabernacle at the rear westward: The bar system on all three sides: south (5) + north (5) + west (5) = 15 bars of acacia wood, all overlaid with gold. The horizontal bars give the tabernacle its structural rigidity — without them the vertical frames could splay outward under load.
33: And he made the middle bar (hab'riach hattichon) to run from end to end halfway up the frames: The middle bar (tichon = middle/central, from the root for middle) is the key structural element — a single long bar running the entire length of each side, mid-height. The two outer bars run only half the length; the central bar is the structural spine. The assembly of the tabernacle in the field required precise coordination of frames and bars — a sophisticated engineering operation for a portable structure.
34: He overlaid the frames with gold and made their rings of gold for holders for the bars and overlaid the bars with gold: The acacia frames and bars are all gold-overlaid; even the structural members visible only within the frame assembly are covered with gold. There are no hidden corners in YHWH's dwelling — the holiness requirement extends to the invisible structural elements as much as the visible furnishings. The rings (ta'ba'ot) through which the bars pass are also gold.
35: He made the veil (parokhet) of blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen; he made it with cherubim skillfully worked (ma'aseh choshev): The parokhet = the inner veil (from a root meaning to divide/separate — it divides the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place). The same three colors as the outer curtains plus fine linen; cherubim woven in (not embroidered — ma'aseh choshev indicates woven designs). This is the curtain torn in two at Jesus's death (Matt 27:51) — the barrier to the divine presence removed by the torn flesh of the High Priest.
36: And he made for it four pillars of acacia and overlaid them with gold. Their hooks were of gold, and he cast for them four bases of silver: The parokhet hangs on four pillars (four = a stable enclosure number), with gold hooks and silver bases. The four silver bases (adnei-kesef) account for four of the 100 silver talent-bases; the inner veil's silver bases are the same material as the wall foundations — the entire inner structure stands on the ransom-silver of the people.
37: He also made a screen (masach) for the entrance of the tent, of blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, embroidered with needlework (ma'aseh roqem): Masach = screen/curtain for the entrance (the outer, accessible curtain — less sacred than the parokhet); ma'aseh roqem = embroidered work (roqem = embroiderer, from raqam = to stitch/embroider in patterns) — a less prestigious weaving technique than the ma'aseh choshev of the inner veil; the entrance screen is embroidered, the inner veil is woven. The gradation of craft level matches the gradation of holiness.
38: And its five pillars with their hooks. He overlaid their capitals (rasheichem) and their fillets (vav = connecting band/hooks) with gold, but their five bases were of bronze (n'choshet): The entrance screen has five pillars (vs. four for the inner veil) with bronze bases (vs. silver). Bronze = the material of judgment and the outer court; silver = the material of the inner zones. The gradient entrance→holy place→most holy place is marked by bronze→silver→gold bases. Rasheichem = their tops/capitals (from rosh = head); vav = a hook or connection-band (also the Hebrew letter vav, which is shaped like a hook).
